text: This twenty-five-year-old boy presented with complaints of left flank pain following a ureteroscopic removal of the stone. The onset of the pain was after removal of the double J stent. His CT scan was done and he was found to have a short segment upper ureteric stricture in the left upper ureter.

He was planned for an end-to-end uretero-uterosomy. A retrograde phylogram was done which showed a tight structure and a guide wire could be negotiated across it with difficulty. Following negotiation of the guide wire, a ureteric catheter was placed and the system proximal to the structure was outlined by contrast and a final view was obtained to determine the exact length of the structure. The ureteric catheter was then left inferior to the stricture to help in its identification during surgery.

The patient is positioned laterally, and three robotic ports are visible along with an assistant pole. The surgeon identifies the ureter after mobilizing the colon, looping it with vessel tape. The ureter is mobilized proximal to the crossing of gonadal vessels, encountering dense adhesions with surrounding fat. The gonadal vessel is freed and repositioned medially to facilitate ureteral dissection. Periureteral fat is carefully teased off the strictural segment of the ureter.

The surgeon identifies the site of the ureteric stricture by advancing a guide wire through the ureteric catheter. The proximal ureter is partially divided, releasing a visible gush of urine. A stay suture is placed on the proximal end before complete division. The distal stricture is divided, and the distal ureter is spatulated. Saline is flushed through the ureteric catheter to confirm patency. Fibrotic portions of the ureter are excised until normal mucosa is exposed, and sutures are placed at both ends of the ureter before excising the intervening fibrous tissue.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis of the ureter using interrupted orovical sutures, placing six sutures circumferentially to join both ends in a watertight manner. A guide wire is passed through the pre-placed ureteric catheter into the pelvic system, followed by the placement of a double J stent. A small drain is also placed and left in dwelling for forty-eight hours.
